analysis: Across the top-ranked heuristics (1–5) a clear template emerges: (1) deterministic nondominated prefiltering, (2) cheap per-point proxies (axis-aligned box volumes or scalarizations) to produce a small, k‑proportional shortlist, (3) a greedy/CELF lazy‑greedy stage that uses cached singleton (and sometimes pairwise) hypervolumes to avoid full recomputation, and (4) bounded local 1‑for‑1 swaps to polish the set. They favor vectorized score computation, stable tie‑breaking, limited shortlist sizes, and explicit caps on swap iterations to control runtime. Weaker variants tend to recompute full hypervolumes repeatedly (nested loops over S and candidates), lack lazy updates or caching, build overly large shortlists or do no shortlisting, and sometimes use unseeded randomization or heavy all‑pairs precomputation that scales poorly. Costly choices seen: full pairwise HV matrices for large L, recomputing HV of the entire set for every candidate/swap, and missing reuse of singleton/pair caches. Good tradeoffs: approximate but fast scoring to prune, lazy greedy to amortize exact HV calls, and small deterministic local search to recover missed improvements.
experience: Prefer small deterministic shortlists from cheap vectorized proxies, cache singleton (and selective pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates to limit exact HV calls, and only verify promising candidates with exact HV. Bound swap iterations and shortlist size. Favor reproducible tie‑breakers and incremental HV updates; avoid full N^2 HV recomputations or dense pairwise HVs unless N is tiny.
current_reflection: Prefer small deterministic shortlists from cheap vectorized proxies, cache singleton (and selective pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates to limit exact HV calls, and only verify promising candidates with exact HV. Bound swap iterations and shortlist size. Favor reproducible tie‑breakers and incremental HV updates; avoid full N^2 HV recomputations or dense pairwise HVs unless N is tiny.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Prefer deterministic shortlisting + a lazy-greedy core: (1) fast Pareto filter, (2) cheap vectorized scoring to pick a small candidate pool ≈ O(k)–O(k·log k), (3) CELF-style greedy using cached singleton HVs and current_hv to avoid repeated full HV recomputes, (4) bounded local swaps that only recompute HV for affected replacements. Tune shortlist size proportional to k and cache everything.

Focus on deterministic Pareto prefiltering, build a shortlist scaled to k via fast box/weight proxies, then run CELF lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs and timestamps; compute exact HV only on shortlist, and finish with a bounded 1-for-1 swap loop. Use sets for lookups, vectorized diffs, and precompute acos/pairwise HVs only if shortlist is tiny. This keeps quality high and runtime predictable.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: deterministic shortlist, vectorized proxies, CELF lazy greedy, cached singleton HVC, incremental HV deltas, bounded swaps, reproducible tie‑breakers
- Advice: Prefilter dominated points; build a small O(k) candidate pool via few weight/box proxies; run CELF lazy greedy using cached singleton HVCs and current_hv, verify only top candidates with exact HV, bound shortlist size and swap iterations, seed ties and always return k
- Avoid: full N^2 HV recomputes or dense pairwise caches unless N is tiny; nested per-candidate Python HV loops; unbounded swaps; nondeterministic tie‑breaking
- Explanation: Cuts expensive exact HV calls via vectorized shortlisting and cached incremental updates, yielding predictable runtime and high-quality selections with minimal verification
